title: Chilled Somen in Roasted Doenjang Dashi
tagline: Summer noodles from an Edo that learned its soybean paste in occupied Seoul.
scenario: Toyotomi Hideyoshi invaded Korea twice in the 1590s, and in our timeline his death in September 1598 ended the war overnight: the garrisons sailed home and Japan turned inward. Here he lives another eleven years. The exhausted Ming buy peace in 1599, the peninsula south of the Han becomes a Toyotomi province, and occupation settles into administration. What Japan took home in our history was potters, whole villages of them, resettled in Kyushu to found the porcelain kilns. In this timeline the resettlement program takes the jang masters too, the brewers of Korea's fermented soybean pastes, and by the 1620s doenjang breweries in Sakai are underselling the miso guilds with a paste that is rougher, deeper, and beefier than anything in Japan. Kyoto's summer somen culture does what it always does with a new ingredient: it chills it. The paste is roasted over charcoal to tame its funk, whisked into iced kombu and katsuobushi dashi, and finished with sour salted plum and perilla, the one herb both kitchens already shared.
method: Make the dashi: steep the kombu in the cold water for 30 minutes, then bring just to the edge of a simmer and remove the kombu. Off the heat, add the katsuobushi, wait 3 minutes, and strain without pressing. Chill completely.

Roast the doenjang: spread the paste 5 mm thick on a small sheet of foil and broil for 3-4 minutes, until the edges darken and it smells nutty and faintly of char. In this timeline it is done over binchotan; a broiler forgives.

Whisk the broth: loosen the roasted paste with a ladle of dashi until smooth, then whisk it back into the rest along with the vinegar and soy sauce. It should taste too strong at room temperature; ice will thin it. Chill until very cold.

Make the perilla-scallion oil: warm the oil with the scallions in a small pan until they just sizzle, about 2 minutes, then pull off the heat and stir in the perilla. Let it steep while everything chills.
Cook the somen: boil for 90 seconds, drain, and rinse hard under cold running water, rubbing the noodles until the water runs clear and they feel squeaky. Drain well and coil into nests.

Serve: set the nests in wide bowls, ladle the cold broth around, and drop in a few ice cubes. Top with torn umeboshi, cucumber, a spoonful of the oil with its solids, and the sesame. Eat before the ice wins.

queries: "doenjang guksu" OR "doenjang noodle soup" cold recipe
verification.verdict: Doenjang guksu exists as a traditional hot noodle dish with the paste dissolved in anchovy broth, and Korea's cold noodle soups (kongguksu, dongchimi guksu) use soy milk or kimchi brine, never doenjang. Japanese cold somen uses mentsuyu. No published recipe chills a roasted-doenjang kombu-katsuobushi dashi or pairs it with umeboshi and perilla oil; perilla-oil noodles exist separately as a Korean dry-tossed dish.
